SQ1 CSP Cale Method (Cubeshape Parity)

SQ1 CSP法 (复形奇偶)

Cale Schoon

Parity Definition 奇偶的定义

Parity refers to the oddness/evenness of the number of swaps required to solve a puzzle. For example, an Adj swap has odd parity, since it requires one swap, and an H-Perm is of even parity since it requires 2. Notice that when you make a slice turn of the puzzle misaligned by 1,0, you swap 2 pairs of corners and 2 pairs of edges, making 4 swaps. Therefore, keeping the puzzle in CS will maintain the puzzle in even parity.

Strange things happen when SQ1 is not in cubeshape. For example, setup SQ1 with the moves /33/12/2-2. Notice that by doing a slice move (/) here, you swap 3 pairs of corners, resulting in 3 swaps and creating odd parity. Then, when you reverse the moves above (-22/-1-2/-3-3), you will have a parity error, since you cannot return the puzzle to even parity by simply keeping it in cubeshape.

/33/12/2-2/-22/-1-2/-3-3/

Parity Determination 确定奇偶

Use binary addition. Check the first 3 yellow edges in the order. Eliminate 3 edges to 2. If it is odd parity case, count 1. If not, count 0. Do the same thing with white edges, yellow corners and white corners. Count the number of yellow edges in the 4 odd numbered edge positions. Do the same thing with yellow corners (check right stickers of corners on U and left stickers of corners on D. Add these 6 parities together. If getting 0, apply even alg. If getting 1, apply odd alg.

parity of the first 3 yellow edges

parity of the first 3 white edges

parity of the first 3 yellow corners

parity of the first 3 white corners

the number of yellow edges in the 4 odd numbered edge positions

the number of yellow corners in the 4 odd numbered edge positions

Odd Parity Cases 奇置换情况

4 Odd Numbered Edge Positions 四个奇数位置的棱块

CSP Algorithms

If the mirror of shape is itself, we have 2 cases.

even

odd

If the mirror of shape is not itself, we have 4 cases.

even

odd

even mirror

odd mirror

Consistency

All my CSP algs are aimed at consistency. I follow the priciple that the counting position for the all cases with the same shape should always be the same. So you can always start at the exact same piece for a Square, Barrel, Pawns and so on. Only exception are Fists as they are easy to count from every position and it’s easy to remember where the two counting groups are. This has the advantage that less time is spent until you can start counting and don’t have to think that much. On the other hand, many of the counting positions are not where you will do the first / so you need to remember where you have to slice. (Edit: Having finished learning CSP now I definitely think that this was the right thing to do, so much easier and knowing where to slice is actually really easy when you already knew optimal CS before). I don’t really know where to slice exactly in most cases without thinking but I know into which shapes I slice into which is enough information for me. Some cases turned out much better and easier this way (normal alg is the even one, longer one is the odd) but some cases are worse. Overall the good cases are in the majority I think. A bad one e.g. is Left and Right 4-2/Line where the odd one is the easier.

Counting

"All algs are shown with the counting result that they would actually yield with the shown alignments. However, I sometimes start with an inverted count so the cases are more logical. I’ll explain what I mean by that in the following paragraph.

I tried to be as consistent as possible with left and right shape variants that they follow the same logic. If a Left Fist goes 2-gen to Scallop/Kite then the Right Fist case does so too. For that I had to implement something.

There are often cases where I start with an inverted count so that two shapes (e.g. Left Pawn/Shield and Right Pawn/Shield) follow the same logic. Left Pawn/Shield’s odd alg swaps the three corners to change the parity. On the other hand, Right Pawn/Shield’s even alg also swaps the 3 corners when you start counting as shown in the image. Having the odd alg change the 3 corners is much more logical and easier to remember. So I start with an odd count for Right Pawn/Shield so I can follow the same logic as for Left Pawn/Shield. Whenever this is the case I paint the case title in green and when I have to start inverted I paint it red. I always do that in the beginning. You could obviously do it at the end but I prefer to do it at the beginning.
